name: Learning Project
description: Experimentation is the goal. Encourage trying new patterns, don't punish dead ends. Document learnings over polish.

extras.text: What do you want to learn from building this?

What's the specific hypothesis you're testing? Phrase it as a falsifiable statement: 'I believe that X, because Y. I'll know I'm right when Z.' How will you know if the experiment succeeded or failed?
What do you already know about this problem from past projects? What's the specific gap that this experiment fills?
What would be the most SURPRISING outcome? What would change your mental model completely?

specSections.title: Learning Goals
specSections.placeholder: _What specific questions will this experiment answer? What knowledge gap does it fill? How will you know if the experiment succeeded or failed?_

specSections.title: Hypothesis
specSections.placeholder: _State a falsifiable hypothesis: 'I believe X because Y. I will know I am right when Z.' If the experiment is complete, state the verdict: confirmed, rejected, or inconclusive._

specSections.title: Experiment Log
specSections.placeholder: _Document what was tried, in what order, and what each attempt produced. Include dead ends — they are data. Tag mental model shifts with EUREKA:_

reminders: Dead ends are acceptable — document why
Try new patterns freely, polish later

EUREKA MOMENT: when your mental model shifts — when you realize something is fundamentally different than you assumed — tag it explicitly: 'EUREKA: [what changed]'. These are the most valuable learnings. Capture them in real time.
Cross-spec learning: before starting, review learnings from similar past specs using `noskills spec list` and `noskills learn search`. Build on what you already know.
Learning velocity matters: if you're 3 iterations in and the hypothesis is still unclear, stop and restate it. Confusion is data — it means the experiment design is wrong.
Negative results are results. A failed experiment with clear documentation is more valuable than a successful experiment with no documentation.
The learning is worth more than the code. If you have to choose between a clean implementation and a documented learning, document the learning.
acceptanceCriteria: At least one learning captured with `noskills spec learn` (one-time) or `noskills learn --rule` (permanent)
Eureka moments tagged separately with 'EUREKA:' prefix in the learning
Hypothesis explicitly stated at the start and evaluated at the end: confirmed, rejected, or inconclusive
Dead ends documented: why each failed approach was tried and why it was abandoned

reviewDimensions.label: Learning Goal Clarity
reviewDimensions.prompt: For each learning goal: what specific question will be answered? How will you know if the experiment succeeded or failed? What's the 'good enough to learn from' threshold?

reviewDimensions.label: Hypothesis Evaluation
reviewDimensions.prompt: Was the hypothesis stated at the start? Was it falsifiable (a clear yes/no outcome)? What was the actual outcome — confirmed, rejected, or inconclusive? If inconclusive: why? What would make it decidable? Were there confounding variables?

reviewDimensions.label: Eureka Moment Capture
reviewDimensions.prompt: What changed in your mental model during this experiment? What did you believe at the start that turned out to be wrong? What surprised you most? Rate the learning density: 0 = built something, learned nothing | 5 = validated a known hypothesis | 10 = mental model completely revised. If score is below 5, why?

reviewDimensions.label: Knowledge Transfer
reviewDimensions.prompt: Are the learnings written for your future self — 6 months from now, context forgotten? Are there code comments that explain WHY a choice was made, not just WHAT? Would a new team member reading the experiment log understand what was tried, why, and what was learned?
